text: Required Models 2 4: - K(-xi, xj) = K(xi, -xj) - K(-xi, -xj) = K(xi, xj) RBF kernel K(xi, xj) = exp(-‚ÄĖxi - xj‚ÄĖ¬≤/2ŌÉ¬≤) satisfies conditions: - K(-xi, -xj) = exp(-‚ÄĖ-xi-(-xj)‚ÄĖ¬≤/2ŌÉ¬≤) = exp(-‚ÄĖxi-xj‚ÄĖ¬≤/2ŌÉ¬≤) = K(xi,xj) ‚úď - K(-xi, xj) = exp(-‚ÄĖ-xi-xj‚ÄĖ¬≤/2ŌÉ¬≤) = exp(-‚ÄĖxi+xj‚ÄĖ¬≤/2ŌÉ¬≤) = K(xi,-xj) ‚úď Polynomial kernels even degree also satisfy Assumption 3.

title: psvr ‚ÄĒ Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: QP solver: osqp package (Models 1 & 2). Supports sparse matrices, commercial license. Linear solver: base::solve() (Models 3 & 4). Dense (N+1)√ó(N+1) system, extra dep. Target validation: fit() functions call stopifnot((y > 0)). Kernel interface: make_kernel(type, ...) returns closure function(xi, xj) type ‚ąą {"rbf", "linear", "polynomial"}. Shared across four models. Yőď storage: stored vector y^2 / gamma; added diagonal via diag(Omega) <- diag(Omega) + y_gamma ‚ÄĒ never materializes full N√óN diagonal matrix. "psvr_mape" (Model 1) "psvr_mape_sym" (Model 2) "psvr_rmspe" (Model 3) "psvr_rmspe_sym" (Model 4) S3 methods: predict.psvr_*() class. Documentation: roxygen2 @param, @return, @examples. Style: tidyverse / base R style: snake_case, <- assignment. License: MIT.

title: Electricity Demand Forecasting with Percentage-Error SVR
text: Electricity grid operators must predict demand days ahead schedule generation assets, balance grid, avoid costly emergency purchases. Victoria, Australia operates competitive spot market forecast errors translate directly monetary penalties ‚ÄĒ making relative accuracy natural objective. vic_elec dataset tsibbledata package records half-hourly demand temperature readings state Victoria 2012‚Äď2014. aggregate daily level: total daily demand (MWh) mean daily temperature (¬įC). Daily forecasting common planning horizon unit commitment fuel scheduling. 1 Sum 48 half-hourly readings get total daily demand MWh. 2 Average temperature across day. 3 Holiday vic_elec logical vector ‚ÄĒ (Holiday) TRUE half-hour day flagged public holiday. 4 Squared temperature capture U-shaped demand‚Äďtemperature relationship (high AC demand summer, high heating demand winter). 5 week_start = 1 ‚Üí Monday = 1, Sunday = 7. Figure¬†1: Daily electricity demand Victoria, 2012‚Äď2014. Colour encodes mean daily temperature. summer (Dec‚ÄďFeb) heat-wave spikes lower weekend/holiday baseline visible. MAPE matters Absolute-error objectives (MAE, MSE) treat days equally. grid operator‚Äôs cost function relative: 10,000 MWh error mild autumn day (baseline ‚Čą 165,000 MWh) 6.1% ‚ÄĒ critical: error large relative scheduled capacity. 10,000 MWh error hot summer day (baseline ‚Čą 305,000 MWh) 3.3% ‚ÄĒ tolerable: dwarfed total load. Minimising MAPE directly aligns model operator‚Äôs penalty structure. Figure¬†2: fixed 10,000 MWh absolute error different relative impact depending day‚Äôs baseline demand.
code:
elec_daily <- vic_elec |> as_tibble() |> mutate(Date = as.Date(Time)) |> group_by(Date) |> summarise( 1 Demand = sum(Demand, na.rm = TRUE), 2 Temperature = mean(Temperature, na.rm = TRUE), 3 IsHoliday = any(Holiday), .groups = "drop" ) |> mutate( 4 Temperature2 = Temperature^2, 5 DayOfWeek = wday(Date, week_start = 1), Month = month(Date), IsWeekend = as.integer(DayOfWeek >= 6), IsHoliday = as.integer(IsHoliday) ) cat(sprintf("Rows: %d | Dates: %s ‚Äď %s ", nrow(elec_daily), min(elec_daily$Date), max(elec_daily$Date))) cat(sprintf("Demand range: %.0f ‚Äď %.0f MWh/day ", min(elec_daily$Demand), max(elec_daily$Demand))) cat(sprintf("Public holidays: %d | Weekend days: %d ", sum(elec_daily$IsHoliday), sum(elec_daily$IsWeekend))) Rows: 1097 | Dates: 2011-12-31 ‚Äď 2014-12-31 Demand range: 82532 ‚Äď 351460 MWh/day Public holidays: 58 | Weekend days: 314 p_ts <- elec_daily |> ggplot(aes(x = Date, y = Demand / 1e3, colour = Temperature, text = paste0("Date: ", Date, "
Demand: ", round(Demand / 1e3, 1), " GWh", "
Temp: ", round(Temperature, 1), "¬įC"))) + geom_line(linewidth = 0.4, alpha = 0.85) + scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "C", name = "Temp (¬įC)") + scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) + labs(x = NULL, y = "Demand (GWh/day)", title = "Victoria daily electricity demand ‚ÄĒ 2012‚Äď2014", subtitle = "Colour = mean daily temperature") + theme_bw() + theme(legend.position = "right") ggplotly(p_ts, tooltip = "text") |> layout(hoverlabel = list(bgcolor = "white"), width = NULL, height = 450) tibble( baseline = seq(80e3, 360e3, by = 1000), abs_error = 10000, pct_error = abs_error / baseline * 100 ) |> ggplot(aes(baseline / 1e3, pct_error)) + geom_line(colour = vpal[2], linewidth = 1.2) + geom_point( data = tibble( baseline = c(165e3, 305e3), pct_error = 10000 / c(165e3, 305e3) * 100 ), aes(baseline / 1e3, pct_error), size = 3, colour = vpal[5] ) + annotate("text", x = 165, y = 10000 / 165e3 * 100 + 0.5, label = "Mild day: 6.1%", hjust = 0, size = 3.5) + annotate("text", x = 305, y = 10000 / 305e3 * 100 + 0.5, label = "Hot day: 3.3%", hjust = 1, size = 3.5) + labs(x = "Baseline demand (GWh/day)", y = "Percentage error (%)", title = "Asymmetric impact of a fixed 10 GWh error", subtitle = "Same absolute error ‚ÄĒ very different relative cost") + scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) + scale_x_continuous(labels = comma)

title: Electricity Demand Forecasting with Percentage-Error SVR
text: Classical regression minimises residuals globally. SVR takes different approach: finds flattest function (smallest ‚ÄĖŌČ‚ÄĖ¬≤) fits training points within őĶ-tube. Points outside tube penalised, points ‚ÄĒ support vectors ‚ÄĒ determine solution. Figure¬†3: SVR 1-D synthetic dataset. Grey band: őĶ-insensitivity tube. Blue points: support vectors (outside boundary). fitted line determined support vectors. Mathematical detail: SVR primal formulation standard őĶ-SVR solves primal problem: minŌČ,b,őĺ,őĺ*12‚ą•ŌČ‚ą•2+C‚ąĎk=1N(őĺk+őĺk*) \min_{\omega, b, \xi, \xi^*} \quad \tfrac{1}{2}\|\omega\|^2   + C \sum_{k=1}^N (\xi_k + \xi_k^*) subject yk‚ąíŌČ‚ä§Ōē(xk)‚ąíb‚Č§őĶ+őĺkŌČ‚ä§Ōē(xk)+b‚ąíyk‚Č§őĶ+őĺk*őĺk,őĺk*‚Č•0 \begin{aligned} y_k - \omega^\top \phi(x_k) - b &\leq \varepsilon + \xi_k \\ \omega^\top \phi(x_k) + b - y_k &\leq \varepsilon + \xi_k^* \\ \xi_k,\, \xi_k^* &\geq 0 \end{aligned} őĶ-tube means residuals smaller őĶ incur zero loss ‚ÄĒ outliers contribute objective. dual reveals solution f(x)=‚ąĎkő≤kK(xk,x)+bf(x) = \sum_k \beta_k K(x_k, x) + b ő≤k=őĪk‚ąíőĪk*\beta_k = \alpha_k - \alpha_k^*.
code: set.seed(7) n_syn  <- 40 x_syn  <- sort(runif(n_syn, 0, 10)) y_syn  <- 2 * x_syn + 1 + rnorm(n_syn, sd = 1.5)  df_syn   <- data.frame(x = x_syn, y = y_syn) svm_1d   <- ksvm(y ~ x, data = df_syn, type = "eps-svr",                  kernel = "vanilladot", C = 5, epsilon = 1.5, scaled = FALSE) Setting default kernel parameters pred_syn <- predict(svm_1d, df_syn) sv_flags <- seq_len(n_syn) %in% SVindex(svm_1d)  tibble(x = x_syn, y = y_syn, yhat = as.numeric(pred_syn), is_sv = sv_flags) |>   ggplot(aes(x, y)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = yhat - 1.5, ymax = yhat + 1.5),               fill = "grey85", alpha = 0.6) +   geom_line(aes(y = yhat), colour = vpal[2], linewidth = 1) +   geom_point(aes(colour = is_sv, size = is_sv), alpha = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "grey60", "TRUE" = vpal[5]),                       labels = c("Interior point", "Support vector"),                       name = NULL) +   scale_size_manual(values = c("FALSE" = 2, "TRUE" = 3.5), guide = "none") +   labs(x = "x", y = "y",        title = "SVR: fitting within the őĶ-tube",        subtitle = "Only support vectors (blue) influence the decision boundary") +   theme(legend.position = "top")

title: Electricity Demand Forecasting with Percentage-Error SVR
text: Six features describe day: temperature (linear quadratic), day week, month, weekend flag, public holiday flag. use strict temporal split ‚ÄĒ 2012‚Äď2013 training, 2014 testing ‚ÄĒ avoid look-ahead bias. 1 strict temporal split: shuffling, future data never touches training. 2 colMeans sd computed training data , applied identically test set. safe way standardize. 3 Raw daily demand (~160,000‚Äď305,000 MWh) divided 1e5 give scaled targets ~1.6‚Äď3.5. Predictions model multiplied 1e5 recover MWh units metric evaluation. Figure¬†5: Train/test temporal split. grey band marks 2014 (test set). Colour encodes IsWeekend. Temporal leakage split calendar year, never shuffling. random split allow 2014 observations leak training, giving optimistically biased test metrics reflect operational forecast accuracy.
code:
feature_cols <- c("Temperature", "Temperature2", "DayOfWeek",                   "Month", "IsWeekend", "IsHoliday")  1train <- elec_daily |> filter(year(Date) <= 2013) test  <- elec_daily |> filter(year(Date) == 2014)  X_raw_tr <- as.matrix(train[, feature_cols]) y_tr     <- train$Demand X_raw_te <- as.matrix(test[, feature_cols]) y_te     <- test$Demand  2# Standardise features using training statistics only cm   <- colMeans(X_raw_tr) cs   <- apply(X_raw_tr, 2, sd) X_tr <- scale(X_raw_tr, center = cm, scale = cs) X_te <- scale(X_raw_te, center = cm, scale = cs)  3# Scale targets to ~ [1.6, 3.5] for numerical conditioning y_scale <- 1e5 y_tr_s  <- y_tr / y_scale y_te_s  <- y_te / y_scale  cat(sprintf("Train: %d days  (%s ‚Äď %s)
",             nrow(train), min(train$Date), max(train$Date))) cat(sprintf("Test:  %d days  (%s ‚Äď %s)

title: Electricity Demand Forecasting with Percentage-Error SVR
text: tutorial demonstrated psvr package real-world daily electricity demand forecasting task using three years data Victoria, Australia. four percentage-error SVR models ‚ÄĒ LS-SVR RMSPE (Model 3), őĶ-SVR MAPE (Model 1), symmetric kernel extensions (Models 2 4) ‚ÄĒ fitted 2012‚Äď2013 data evaluated full 2014 hold-year. percentage-error models achieve MAPE values 3.6‚Äď4.1%, outperforming classical őĶ-SVR baseline (MAPE 4.1%) RMSPE-minimising models. Model 3 (LS-SVR RMSPE) achieves best MAPE 3.58%, confirming theoretical motivation: relative accuracy matters ‚ÄĒ electricity markets forecast errors penalised proportionally ‚ÄĒ aligning loss function forecasting objective measurably improves performance. symmetric kernel extensions (Models 2 4) provide principled way inject structural constraint model. dataset, Model 2 slightly improves upon Model 1 MAPE, Model 4 marginally Model 3 ‚ÄĒ illustrating value symmetry constraint depends whether aligns data‚Äôs geometry standardized feature space. practice, symmetry type (‚ąą{‚ąí1,1}\\{-1,1\}) hyperparameters selected via time-series cross-validation (e.g., rolling-window scheme) respect temporal structure data. Full mathematical derivations, KKT conditions, proofs unified framework available Benavides-Herrera et al.¬†(2026, review). psvr package provides production-ready R implementations four models together tidymodels integration via parsnip seamless use modelling pipelines.

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Classical SVR minimises absolute-error losses (MAE, MSE), misaligned scale-free accuracy criteria standard forecasting. error 1 unit negligible target 1 000 large 2. psvr implements four SVR variants derived percentage-error loss functions (Benavides-Herrera et al., 2026): models require strictly positive targets (y > 0), condition percentage residuals well-defined.

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: reproduce experiments reported Tables 1‚Äď2 paper using Boston Housing dataset (Harrison & Rubinfeld, 1978). 506 observations strictly positive median home values (MEDV, thousands USD), making MAPE well-defined throughout.
code: library(psvr) library(ggplot2)  # Boston Housing is available in the MASS package data("Boston", package = "MASS")  # Target: median home value (all > 0) y_all <- Boston$medv X_raw <- as.matrix(Boston[, setdiff(names(Boston), "medv")])  stopifnot(all(y_all > 0)) cat("N =", nrow(X_raw), "  p =", ncol(X_raw),     "  y range: [", round(min(y_all), 1), ",", round(max(y_all), 1), "]\n") #> N = 506   p = 13   y range: [ 5 , 50 ]

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Features standardised using training-set statistics RBF kernel operates comparable scale across 13 predictors.
code: set.seed(42) n      <- nrow(X_raw) tr_idx <- sample(n, floor(0.7 * n))  X_raw_tr <- X_raw[tr_idx, ];  y_tr <- y_all[tr_idx] X_raw_te <- X_raw[-tr_idx, ]; y_te <- y_all[-tr_idx]  # Standardise: centre and scale by training mean/sd col_mean <- colMeans(X_raw_tr) col_sd   <- apply(X_raw_tr, 2, sd) X_tr <- scale(X_raw_tr, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd) X_te <- scale(X_raw_te, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd)

title: Getting Started with psvr
code: mape  <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat) / y) * 100 rmspe <- function(y, yhat) sqrt(mean(((y - yhat) / y)^2)) * 100 r2    <- function(y, yhat) 1 - sum((y - yhat)^2) / sum((y - mean(y))^2)

code: lm_df_tr <- as.data.frame(X_tr) lm_df_te <- as.data.frame(X_te) lm_fit   <- lm(y_tr ~ ., data = lm_df_tr) lm_pred  <- predict(lm_fit, newdata = lm_df_te)  cat(sprintf("Linear regression ‚ÄĒ MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R¬≤: %.4f\n",             mape(y_te, lm_pred), rmspe(y_te, lm_pred), r2(y_te, lm_pred))) #> Linear regression ‚ÄĒ MAPE: 16.29%  RMSPE: 22.48%  R¬≤: 0.7730

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: LS-SVR formulation replaces QP linear system using quadratic penalty percentage residuals. dual reduces : [0ūĚüŹ‚ä§ūĚüŹő©+Yőď][būĚõā]=[0ūĚź≤] \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \mathbf{1}^\top \\ \mathbf{1} & \Omega + Y_\Gamma \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} b \\ \boldsymbol{\alpha} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \mathbf{y} \end{bmatrix} Yőď=diag(y12/őď,‚Ä¶,yN2/őď)Y_\Gamma = \operatorname{diag}(y_1^2/\Gamma, \ldots, y_N^2/\Gamma).
code: # make_kernel() returns a closure K(xi, xj) = exp(-||xi - xj||^2 / (2 sigma^2)) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)  # gamma = 5000: regularisation; larger gamma -> smaller Y_Gamma diagonal -> tighter fit fit_ls <- rmspe_lssvr(X_tr, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 5000) pred_ls <- predict(fit_ls, X_te)  cat(sprintf("LS-SVR RMSPE  ‚ÄĒ MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R¬≤: %.4f\n",             mape(y_te, pred_ls), rmspe(y_te, pred_ls), r2(y_te, pred_ls))) #> LS-SVR RMSPE  ‚ÄĒ MAPE: 15.46%  RMSPE: 27.21%  R¬≤: 0.7277 print(fit_ls) #>  #> LS-SVR with RMSPE loss  [psvr_rmspe] #>  #>   Kernel:        RBF (sigma = 1) #>   Gamma:         5000 #>   Training obs.: 354 cf_ls <- coef(fit_ls) # alpha: N dual variables; weight each training point's kernel contribution #        in f(x) = sum_k alpha_k K(x_k, x) + b (all N points, no sparsity) # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  all N training inputs stored for prediction cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]\n",             cf_ls$b, min(cf_ls$alpha), max(cf_ls$alpha))) #> b = 22.5268  |  alpha range: [-59.7536, 43.7327]

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: őĶ-SVR formulation optimises QP sample-dependent box constraints |ő≤k|‚Č§100C/yk|\beta_k| \le 100C/y_k: tighter bounds small targets, concentrating model capacity low-magnitude observations.
code:
# C = 10: per-sample box bound |beta_k| <= 100*C/y_k; eps = 1: tube width (% of y_k) fit_ep <- mape_svr(X_tr, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 1) pred_ep <- predict(fit_ep, X_te)  cat(sprintf("őĶ-SVR MAPE    ‚ÄĒ MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R¬≤: %.4f
",             mape(y_te, pred_ep), rmspe(y_te, pred_ep), r2(y_te, pred_ep))) #> őĶ-SVR MAPE    ‚ÄĒ MAPE: 15.77%  RMSPE: 27.54%  R¬≤: 0.7617 cat(sprintf("Support vectors: %d / %d
", length(fit_ep$beta), nrow(X_tr))) #> Support vectors: 328 / 354 print(fit_ep) #>  #> Epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss  [psvr_mape] #>  #>   Kernel:          RBF (sigma = 1) #>   C:               10 #>   eps:             1 #>   Training obs.:   354 #>   Support vectors: 328 (92.7%) cf_ep <- coef(fit_ep) # alpha: beta_k = alpha_k - alpha_k* for each support vector; non-zero only for #        training points outside the percentage-error őĶ-tube (sparse) # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  training rows corresponding to support vectors only cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]
",             cf_ep$b, min(cf_ep$alpha), max(cf_ep$alpha))) #> b = 23.1097  |  alpha range: [-111.5553, 82.6446]

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: vignette verifies R implementations psvr reproduce numerical results reported Tables 1‚Äď2 Benavides-Herrera et al.¬†(2026), originally computed Python using cvxopt (Models 1 & 2) numpy.linalg.lstsq (Models 3 & 4). verification strategy : Use dataset, split seed, hyperparameters Python experiments. Report four metrics: MAPE, RMSPE, R¬≤, MSE test set. Compare R outputs paper reference values. Small numerical differences expected acceptable: Python implementation uses cvxopt QP (Models 1 & 2) pseudoinverse via numpy.linalg.lstsq linear system (Models 3 & 4), whereas psvr uses osqp base::solve(), respectively. approaches solve mathematical problem use different numerical algorithms different stopping tolerances, can produce discrepancies order 0.01‚Äď0.1 percentage points reported metrics.

title: psvr ‚ÄĒ Percentage-Error SVR
text: psvr implements four support vector regression models derived unified mathematical framework percentage-error loss functions. Classical SVR minimises absolute-error losses (MAE, MSE), scale-dependent: error 1 unit negligible target 1 000 critical 2. psvr addresses optimising MAPE RMSPE directly, making well-suited forecasting tasks targets strictly positive relative accuracy matters. models require strictly positive targets (y > 0).

title: psvr ‚ÄĒ Percentage-Error SVR
code: # Development version from GitHub remotes::install_github("pbenavidesh/psvr")

title: psvr ‚ÄĒ Percentage-Error SVR
text: full worked example Boston Housing dataset ‚ÄĒ including 70/30 train‚Äďtest split, feature standardisation, comparison linear baseline ‚ÄĒ see vignette("getting-started", package = "psvr").
code:
library(psvr)  # Synthetic dataset: even function (f(-x) = f(x)), targets strictly positive set.seed(42) n  <- 100 X  <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2) y  <- 2 + X[, 1]^2 + 0.5 * X[, 2]^2 + rnorm(n, sd = 0.1)  tr <- 1:70;  te <- 71:100 X_tr <- X[tr, ];  y_tr <- y[tr] X_te <- X[te, ];  y_te <- y[te]  # Standardise features using training-set statistics (important for RBF) col_mean <- colMeans(X_tr);  col_sd <- apply(X_tr, 2, sd) X_tr_s   <- scale(X_tr, col_mean, col_sd) X_te_s   <- scale(X_te, col_mean, col_sd)  K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)  # Model 1 ‚ÄĒ őĶ-SVR with MAPE fit1  <- mape_svr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 0.5, eps = 5) pred1 <- predict(fit1, X_te_s)  # Model 2 ‚ÄĒ Symmetric őĶ-SVR with MAPE  (a = 1: even-function prior) fit2  <- mape_sym_svr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 0.5, eps = 5, a = 1) pred2 <- predict(fit2, X_te_s)  # Model 3 ‚ÄĒ LS-SVR with RMSPE fit3  <- rmspe_lssvr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 100) pred3 <- predict(fit3, X_te_s)  # Model 4 ‚ÄĒ Symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE  (a = 1: even-function prior) fit4  <- rmspe_sym_lssvr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 100, a = 1) pred4 <- predict(fit4, X_te_s)  mape <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat) / y) * 100  cat(sprintf("Model 1 (MAPE e-SVR):       MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred1))) cat(sprintf("Model 2 (MAPE sym e-SVR):   MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred2))) cat(sprintf("Model 3 (RMSPE LS-SVR):     MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred3))) cat(sprintf("Model 4 (RMSPE sym LS-SVR): MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred4))) #> Model 1 (MAPE e-SVR):       MAPE = 3.59% #> Model 2 (MAPE sym e-SVR):   MAPE = 6.04% #> Model 3 (RMSPE LS-SVR):     MAPE = 3.76% #> Model 4 (RMSPE sym LS-SVR): MAPE = 5.79%

title: psvr 0.0.0.9001
text: Expanded parsnip integration 4 12 model specs following tidymodels pattern used svm_rbf(), svm_poly(), svm_linear(): psvr_mape_rbf(), psvr_mape_poly(), psvr_mape_linear() ‚ÄĒ Model 1 specs. psvr_mape_sym_rbf(), psvr_mape_sym_poly(), psvr_mape_sym_linear() ‚ÄĒ Model 2 specs. psvr_rmspe_rbf(), psvr_rmspe_poly(), psvr_rmspe_linear() ‚ÄĒ Model 3 specs. psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf(), psvr_rmspe_sym_poly(), psvr_rmspe_sym_linear() ‚ÄĒ Model 4 specs. Kernel parameters now tunable parsnip args mapped existing dials params: rbf_sigma ‚Üí dials::rbf_sigma(), degree ‚Üí dials::degree(), scale_factor ‚Üí dials::scale_factor(). kernel closure built inside fit wrapper longer appears parsnip layer. old single-spec API (psvr_mape(), psvr_mape_sym(), psvr_rmspe(), psvr_rmspe_sym()) removed. Migrate replacing, example, psvr_rmspe(cost = tune()) |> set_engine("psvr", kernel = K) psvr_rmspe_rbf(cost = tune(), rbf_sigma = 1) |> set_engine("psvr").
